quotedTweet.text:
The Story of Mr Crude Part 2

We are entering the 10th week since the Strait of Hormuz has shut. I hear the world is functioning just fine without me so far. More oil found apparently, from China's underground storage and even under US military bases! 

Somehow the mass balance must meet reality. 10-11 mbd of actual crude production shut-in must be met with the same demand destruction because all that oil reserves drawdown can only last you for a few months.

So I think time is ticking. Summer is coming. I am getting a bit hot under my collar. 

Hope Cowboy Crude can do his magic but can he (WTI) really solve the diesel and fuel oil problem globally?

I am going to watch this play out, maybe quietly from the other side of the Strait of Hormuz, shipped away undetected by AIS...

Signing off,
Mr Crude

#oott

Crude Market Flash: China's not taking much June loading Saudi crude

- Saudi allocation for China is heard to be between 12-14 mb, about 3 VLCCs worth lower than May loaders

- This could partly due to a higher OSP, but more likely due to increased SPR access by the state-owned refiners

- Saudi crudes allocated are all Arab Light ex Yanbu as the other grades are unable to load from that port

- It is more likely to see increased buying activity from the Chinese this week, as they had been quiet in the prior week due to the Labour Day holidays

By @JuneGoh_Sparta, Senior Oil Market Analyst.

text:
We need more specific language when talking about "market manipulation," which like most things is a spectrum.

Unprecedented Trump/White House jawboning and verbal interventions: obviously happening, manipulative in that they successfully moves markets, but fair game (if irresponsible) and not what most people mean when they say "manipulation"

Direct intervention by Treasury/etc (e.g., explicitly shorting futures):

lots of accusations, I've seen no proof, whole different ballgame with grave short- and long-term consequences, and imho there are far simpler explanations for crude price action than that.

It cannot be stressed hard enough that Hormuz sovereignty was never a sticking point prior to the war.

This is the reality that was shattered, and we aren't going back.

Iran threatened the Strait plenty, but it was always largely bluster and it's never been closed until now.

quotedTweet.text:
🚨 IRAN DEMANDS CONTROL OF HORMUZ IN WAR TALKS WITH US

Iran has demanded sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz in its latest proposal to the US aimed at ending the war, according to state media.

The key oil shipping route has been effectively blocked since fighting with the US and Israel began in February, disrupting exports from Gulf producers including Saudi Arabia and Kuwait.

Iran and Oman both hold Exclusive Economic Zones in the strait, but under international law, foreign ships retain the right of “innocent passage” through the waterway.

#Silver trades sharply higher, with futures and XAGUSD both trading back above $85 for the first time in two months. The break above $82.20 and $82.70 in SIN6 appears to have triggered momentum and technical buying from leveraged accounts, many of which had been fence-sitting for several weeks. Additional support is coming from the industrial metals space, where copper continues to strengthen after breaking key resistance last week. Shanghai premiums remain relatively subdued, suggesting this is not the primary driver behind the latest rally. So far today, the 0.382 Fibo has been taken out with the 0.50 located at $91.50.

Gold has reluctantly followed silver higher after India’s Prime Minister urged the country’s 1.4 billion citizens to pause gold purchases for at least a year in an effort to ease pressure on foreign-exchange reserves and the rupee.
The move has helped drive the XAUXAG ratio lower towards key support in the 55.50 area, reflecting silver’s current outperformance. Spot gold meanwhile remains rangebound, with support established ahead of USD 4,500, while resistance is seen at the 50-day moving average, currently near USD 4,770, and followed by USD 4850.

Interestingly, the move is occurring on a day with firmer oil prices, potentially signalling a loosening of the higher oil → higher inflation → lower gold narrative that has recently been weighing on bullion.

China’s trade surplus widened sharply in April to $84.8 billion from $51.1 billion in March. Ahead of Donald Trump’s visit this week, China’s exports to the U.S. rose 11.3%, the fastest pace in 15 months.
In #commodities, the sharp drop in crude oil and refined product imports stood out amid the ongoing disruption to Middle East supplies, while soybean imports more than doubled.
Crude oil: 9.4mb/d, -20% m/m & y/y
Refined products: 1.8m tons, -50% m/m & y/y
Soybeans: 8.5m tons, +111% m/m & 39% y/y

#Commodities comment: 
Oil prices climbed after U.S. President Donald Trump rejected Iran’s latest response to his proposal to end the 10-week conflict as “totally unacceptable.” The two sides continue to maintain a fragile ceasefire while the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz drags on. After briefly trading down to USD 96 last week on renewed hopes the strait would reopen, Brent has rebounded above USD 105. Morgan Stanley has described the situation as a “race against time,” warning that recent mitigating factors - such as a surge in U.S. exports (+3.8 mb/d YoY over the past 30 days) and weaker Chinese imports (-5.5 mb/d) - cannot persist indefinitely. That increasingly leaves demand destruction through slower consumption growth and higher prices as the only viable mechanisms to rebalance the market.

All three of the major oil forecasting agencies will publish their monthly market outlooks during the week, starting with EIA on Tuesday, followed by OPEC and IEA on Wednesday.

Gold trades lower after Donald Trump rejected Iran’s latest proposal, lifting both the dollar and U.S. bond yields. Additional pressure came after India’s Prime Minister Narendra Modi urged Indians - the world’s second-largest bullion consumers - to avoid buying gold for at least a year to preserve foreign-exchange reserves as the Middle East war and energy shortages widen India’s trade deficit and weigh on the rupee. Overall, gold remains rangebound, with support established ahead of USD 4,500, while resistance is seen at the 50-day moving average, currently near USD 4,768.

The latest COT report covering the week to 5 May showed continued hedge funds rotation into a rising agricultural sector led by corn, soybeans, sugar, cotton, and cattle. Continued strength in the U.S. beef market lifted the live cattle net long to a 13-month high, and valued at USD 14.4 billion, making it the third-largest hedge fund long after gold and Brent crude. Led by corn and the soybeans complex, the combined crop long surged to a record high since data began in 2006, while CBOT wheat, by contrast, flipped back to a net short as elevated contango continued to favor bearish carry trades.

$100 oil is squeezing American consumers but they're not breaking, they're adapting.

Goldman just cut their US consumer spending forecast twice since January:

• Consumer discretionary cash flow growth slashed from 5.1% to 3.7%
• Energy spending up 14% this year vs normal seasonal patterns
• Bottom income quintile growth: just 0.8% vs 3.7% average
• Low-income households spend 4x more on gas as % of income vs top quintile
• SNAP and Medicaid cuts hitting the same group simultaneously

And here's the kicker: consumers aren't capitulating, they're repositioning.

Private label gaining share as households downtrade on staples. Younger consumers signaling weakening future intent. But experiences and travel stay defended.

The American consumer just showed you their priorities.

Position via ST0x.

Source: Goldman Sachs Consumer Research

The S&P closed at a record high on Friday. Brent crude is above $103. The 10-year is at 4.38% and rising. The Fed's new chair is facing the possibility of hiking, not cutting. By traditional macro logic, long-duration tech should be under significant pressure.

It isn't. That is the signal worth reading.

JPMorgan's global investment strategy head said it plainly on Bloomberg: the fundamental driver is increased capital spending. That sentence contains a regime shift. The market is no longer treating AI as a technology theme. It is treating AI as an independent capital cycle, one with enough momentum to overpower the conventional transmission mechanism of tightening.

The old sequence ran like this: high oil plus high rates produces demand slowdown, which produces tech valuation compression. That sequence assumes the dominant force in the system is monetary policy. What the market is pricing today is different. AI capex is itself generating demand. Compute, data centers, semiconductors, power infrastructure, the buildout is large enough and persistent enough that it is absorbing tightening rather than breaking under it.

This is what capex persistence looks like in a funding stress regime. Three forces are running simultaneously: a supply shock in energy, fiscal expansion, and an AI investment cycle that shows no sign of slowing. Liquidity has not meaningfully loosened. Yet growth assets are being chased. The implicit assumption holding this together is that the energy shock is temporary and AI earnings growth is structural.

That assumption may be correct. But it is worth naming what it is. When markets begin to ignore oil and yields simultaneously, liquidity preference has become very concentrated. The AI cycle and macro tightening have not yet collided. The market is betting on the duration of one outlasting the other.

That is a bullish signal. It is also a late-cycle signal. Both things are true at the same time.

I'm watching daily as Israel 🇮🇱 disables Iranians from being able to pump thfur own oil. 

Imagine Bibi with control of OPEC and Venezuelan oil. 

Got the picture?

Read that again slowly.

12 million barrels per day.

In three weeks.

Saudi Aramco just told the world something the oil market didn't want to hear:

We have an unlimited supply ready to go.

This isn't a production announcement.

It's a warning shot.

To whom?

To every OPEC+ member quietly cheating on quotas.

To every shale producer in Texas, pricing in $80 oil forever.

To every petro-state whose entire national budget runs on crude staying above $70.

Aramco's break-even cost per barrel: ~$3.

Everyone else's: $50–$80.

They can flood the market and profit.

Everyone else drowns.

This is what energy dominance actually looks like — not sanctions, not tariffs, not diplomatic pressure.

Just a calm statement that says:

"We can replace your production in 21 days."

The geopolitical implications are massive:

— Iran's leverage weakens. — Russia's war chest gets squeezed further. — U.S. shale faces an existential pricing floor question. — The energy transition just got quietly accelerated because cheap oil kills the urgency narrative AND the investment case for alternatives simultaneously.

Aramco didn't flex muscles.
They reminded everyone who owns the muscle.

UAE exits OPEC after nearly 60 years, signaling a major shift in global oil politics. This move could reshape crude production decisions and influence energy markets worldwide. A pivotal moment in geopolitics. #OPEC #UAE #Oil #Energy #Geopolitics

@ksorbs Dont forget,Dotard brokered the deal for OPEC+ back in 2020 to cut oil production by 10M bpd which didnt sunset until April 2022. That was a huge part of the inflation spike Biden got blamed for.

🇪🇺 It is not too late.

Europe can still act.

100 km/h for all diesel cars means: ✅ lower oil imports
✅ less dependence on Russia and OPEC
✅ fewer emissions
✅ fewer deadly accidents
✅ faster transition to electric mobility
✅ stronger European energy independence

Europe must become cleaner, safer and more efficient.

Not ideological.
Just common sense.

🇪🇺 Europe drives smarter — not dirtier.
